news_summary: Scientists have developed innovative methods to eliminate persistent chemicals, advanced community-focused sustainable energy strategies, created eco-friendly materials for cold climates, and uncovered previously overlooked European wetland ecosystems through detailed mapping.
news_summary_fr: Les scientifiques ont mis au point des méthodes innovantes pour éliminer les substances chimiques persistantes, élaboré des stratégies énergétiques durables axées sur les communautés, créé des matériaux respectueux de l'environnement adaptés aux climats froids et mis en lumière, grâce à une cartographie détaillée, des écosystèmes de zones humides européens jusqu'alors méconnus.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han desarrollado métodos innovadores para eliminar sustancias químicas persistentes, han impulsado estrategias avanzadas de energía sostenible centradas en la comunidad, han creado materiales respetuosos con el medio ambiente para climas fríos y han descubierto ecosistemas de humedales europeos que antes pasaban desapercibidos gracias a una cartografía detallada.

articles.title: Scientists found two powerful new ways to destroy forever chemicals
articles.summary: Scientists are testing two promising ways to destroy PFAS, the stubborn “forever chemicals” that can accumulate in water and resist normal treatment. One method uses collapsing vapor bubbles to generate extreme heat and reactive molecules, while the other uses cold plasma and rising gas bubbles to pull PFAS to the surface and break them apart.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf (HZDR) are testing two technologies designed to destroy per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), a vast family of industrial chemicals known for resisting natural and conventional breakdown processes.

The first approach uses hydrodynamic cavitation, which creates and collapses tiny vapor bubbles in contaminated water. The second combines cold atmospheric plasma with gas dispersion. Analyses performed by specialists at the Helmholtz Centre for Environmental Research (UFZ) confirmed that PFAS molecules were being degraded and that fluoride was released during treatment.

Once the technologies are sufficiently developed for commercial use, they could help industries treat contaminated wastewater before it reaches rivers, lakes, and oceans.

Why PFAS Are So Difficult to Destroy

Some PFAS are suspected of damaging genetic material and raising cancer risk, although the biological effects of many compounds in this group are still poorly understood. More than 10,000 short-chain and long-chain industrial chemicals are classified as PFAS.

Their extraordinary durability comes from highly stable carbon-fluorine bonds, which are among the strongest bonds found in organic chemistry. This resistance has earned PFAS the nickname "forever chemicals."

PFAS can enter rivers and oceans through wastewater and have now spread across the globe. Researchers have also recently detected high concentrations in the Elbe River, creating a possible health risk for plants, animals, and people.

As part of Germany's "National Water Strategy," which aims to protect water resources and secure the country's drinking water supply, scientists at HZDR are investigating ways to reduce PFAS contamination and systematically destroy the chemicals rather than simply removing or relocating them.

A preliminary study launched in 2022 was led by postdoctoral researcher Dr. Ysabel Huaccallo-Aguilar. Her team examined whether hydrodynamic cavitation could degrade PFAS in water.

Collapsing Bubbles Create Extreme Conditions

"In hydrodynamic cavitation, we pass PFAS-enriched water through a constriction, generating small vapor bubbles," explains Dr. Sebastian Reinecke, head of the Department of Water and Environmental Technologies at HZDR.

Long-chain PFAS behave like surface-active substances, causing them to collect on the bubbles. As the water moves beyond the constricted section, pressure rises and the bubbles violently collapse.

"When the bubbles burst under the rising ambient pressure in the water downstream of the constriction, the PFAS that are attached to the bubbles are exposed to local temperature spikes of several thousand degrees Celsius," Reinecke explains.

Cavitation also creates highly reactive hydroxyl radicals. These molecules react readily with nearby substances and may help destroy compounds produced during the initial stages of PFAS breakdown.

"Our hypothesis is that they attack the intermediate products, significantly boosting PFAS degradation."

Huaccallo-Aguilar and her colleagues demonstrated that cavitation could degrade PFAS in tap water while also mineralizing organically bound fluorine. As treatment continued, the amount of fluoride measured in the water steadily increased, indicating that fluorine was being separated from the original PFAS molecules.

The experiments focused on perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), a well-studied and exceptionally persistent member of the PFAS family. By the conclusion of the test, the process had degraded about 37 percent of the dissolved PFOS molecules while maintaining a stable rate of breakdown.

"We are now conducting follow-up experiments to increase the degradation rate," Reinecke explains. "Our goal is to improve the process to a degradation rate of more than 80 percent of the PFAS in the solution and mineralizing more than 50 percent of the fluorine that is bound in the chemicals -- that means, breaking down the carbon-fluorine bonds that are typical of PFAS."

Cold Plasma Rapidly Attacks PFAS

In a separate set of experiments, environmental engineer Dr. Amit Kumar combined cold atmospheric plasma with gas dispersion to destroy PFAS.

The method works under normal surrounding conditions and does not require catalysts or added chemicals. During his PhD research, Kumar had studied how reactive chemical species produced by plasma could break down micropollutants. He adapted those findings for the PFAS experiments.

"We generated plasma at the water surface while simultaneously introducing gas into the PFAS-contaminated water," says Sebastian Reinecke, explaining the experimental setup. "The PFAS attach to the surface of the gas bubbles. As they rise, the water is constantly circulated. This brings the PFAS to the surface, where they are broken down in the plasma."

The plasma treatment almost completely degraded both long-chain and short-chain PFAS. It also released about 35 percent of the fluorine atoms originally bound within the chemicals, converting them into fluoride salts.

The results were faster than those produced through cavitation, but the method also had important drawbacks.

"While this method has significantly faster reaction kinetics than cavitation, it also consumes far more energy per volume unit," Reinecke notes. "In addition, the process generates numerous transformation products that we have not yet been able to investigate in detail -- for instance, gaseous compounds that form during the reaction."

Additional experiments are underway to determine whether any of these transformation products could pose health risks. The researchers also want to identify ways to prevent potentially hazardous substances from forming.

Combining Cavitation and Plasma

The team is now adapting the plasma system to treat larger amounts of contaminated water. By using multiple electrodes and a technical gas injector, the researchers are increasing the reaction volume from approximately 50 milliliters to five liters.

Their longer-term goal is to combine plasma treatment with hydrodynamic cavitation, bringing the strengths of both technologies into a single system.

"I believe we'll achieve high degradation rates by combining the highly reactive species from the plasma with the effects of cavitation," says Reinecke.

If the combined approach proves successful, it could lead to a new and more efficient technology for destroying PFAS in contaminated water before the chemicals spread into the environment.

This research was funded by the Helmholtz Association's Impulse and Networking Fund via the Clean Water Technology Lab (CLEWATEC), a Helmholtz Innovation Lab, under reference number HIL-A02. The projects "HyKaPro SAB-EFRE" and "Plasma4PFAS SAB-EFRE" are co-financed by the European Union and tax revenue as approved by the Saxon Parliament in its state budget.

This development addresses a major environmental and public health challenge by potentially enabling large-scale removal of persistent pollutants from water sources, aligning with national water protection strategies and promising broad societal and ecological benefits.
articles.personality_title: New technologies show promise in breaking down harmful forever chemicals in water
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – PFAS, often called "forever chemicals," are industrial substances that do not break down easily and have polluted water worldwide. They can harm living things and are hard to remove because of their strong chemical bonds.

**What happened** – Scientists at the Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf in Germany tested two new ways to destroy PFAS in water. One method uses tiny vapor bubbles that form and then collapse, creating extreme heat and special molecules that can break PFAS apart. The other uses cold plasma (a kind of energized gas) combined with gas bubbles to bring PFAS to the surface and break them down. Both methods showed they could reduce PFAS levels and release fluoride, a sign that PFAS molecules were being destroyed.

**Impact** – These methods could change how industries clean wastewater by actually breaking down PFAS instead of just moving them around. The bubble method already destroyed about 37% of a common PFAS chemical, and the plasma method worked even faster but uses more energy. This is important because PFAS pollution threatens rivers, lakes, and drinking water, and current treatments are not effective at removing these chemicals fully.

**What's next step** – Researchers want to improve these techniques to destroy even more PFAS and test if combining both methods works better. They are also checking if any new chemicals made during treatment could be harmful. The goal is to develop a powerful, energy-efficient system to clean large amounts of water before PFAS spread into the environment.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have developed two promising new methods that can break down stubborn PFAS chemicals in water, moving closer to safer and cleaner water sources worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles technologies prometteuses pour détruire les polluants persistants dans l’eau
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les PFAS, surnommés « substances chimiques éternelles », sont des produits industriels qui ne se décomposent pas facilement et contaminent les eaux dans le monde entier. Ils peuvent être dangereux pour les êtres vivants et sont difficiles à éliminer à cause de leurs liaisons chimiques très solides.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques du Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf en Allemagne ont testé deux nouvelles méthodes pour détruire les PFAS dans l’eau. La première utilise de petites bulles de vapeur qui se forment puis éclatent, créant une chaleur extrême et des molécules réactives capables de décomposer les PFAS. La seconde utilise du plasma froid (un gaz énergisé) avec des bulles de gaz pour faire remonter les PFAS à la surface et les casser. Les deux méthodes ont montré qu’elles pouvaient réduire les PFAS et libérer du fluorure, preuve que les molécules PFAS étaient détruites.

**Impact** – Ces méthodes pourraient changer la façon dont les industries traitent les eaux usées en détruisant réellement les PFAS au lieu de simplement les déplacer. La méthode des bulles a déjà détruit environ 37 % d’un PFAS courant, et le plasma fonctionne plus vite mais consomme plus d’énergie. Cela est important car la pollution par les PFAS menace les rivières, les lacs et l’eau potable, et les traitements actuels ne les éliminent pas complètement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent améliorer ces techniques pour détruire encore plus de PFAS et tester une combinaison des deux méthodes. Ils vérifient aussi si des nouveaux produits chimiques formés durant le traitement pourraient être dangereux. Leur but est de créer un système puissant et économe en énergie pour purifier de grandes quantités d’eau avant que les PFAS ne contaminent l’environnement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé deux méthodes prometteuses pour décomposer les PFAS dans l’eau, rapprochant ainsi la possibilité d’une eau plus propre et plus sûre dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas tecnologías prometedoras para eliminar químicos persistentes en el agua
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los PFAS, conocidos como "químicos para siempre", son sustancias industriales que no se descomponen fácilmente y han contaminado el agua en todo el mundo. Pueden dañar a los seres vivos y son difíciles de eliminar debido a sus fuertes enlaces químicos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos del Helmholtz-Zentrum Dresden-Rossendorf en Alemania probaron dos nuevas formas de destruir PFAS en el agua. Un método usa pequeñas burbujas de vapor que se forman y colapsan, creando un calor extremo y moléculas especiales que pueden romper los PFAS. El otro usa plasma frío (un gas energizado) combinado con burbujas de gas para llevar los PFAS a la superficie y descomponerlos. Ambos métodos mostraron que podían reducir los niveles de PFAS y liberar fluoruro, una señal de que las moléculas de PFAS estaban siendo destruidas.

**Impacto** – Estos métodos podrían cambiar cómo las industrias limpian las aguas residuales al destruir los PFAS en lugar de solo moverlos. El método de las burbujas destruyó alrededor del 37 % de un PFAS común, y el plasma funcionó más rápido pero consume más energía. Esto es importante porque la contaminación por PFAS amenaza ríos, lagos y el agua potable, y los tratamientos actuales no eliminan completamente estos químicos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren mejorar estas técnicas para destruir aún más PFAS y probar si combinar ambos métodos funciona mejor. También están revisando si nuevos químicos creados durante el tratamiento podrían ser dañinos. El objetivo es desarrollar un sistema potente y eficiente para limpiar grandes cantidades de agua antes de que los PFAS se extiendan al medio ambiente.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos han desarrollado dos métodos prometedores que pueden descomponer los difíciles PFAS en el agua, acercándonos a fuentes de agua más seguras y limpias en todo el mundo.

articles.title: Sustainability In Your Ear: Cody Two Bears on Indigenized Energy’s Community-Centric Grid Strategy
articles.summary:
Tribal households in the United States carry an energy burden 28% above the national average...
The post Sustainability In Your Ear: Cody Two Bears on Indigenized Energy’s Community-Centric Grid Strategy appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Tribal households in the United States carry an energy burden 28% above the national average and endure 6.5 times more power outages a year, and roughly 17,000 tribal homes — housing at least 54,000 people — have no electricity at all, according to the Department of Energy. Those are the communities now first in line to absorb the next surge in demand, because Energy Information Administration concluded data center load will be the dominant driver of long-term U.S. electricity growth. Our guest this week is Cody Two Bears, founder and CEO of Indigenized Energy, and his answer to that arithmetic is tribal energy sovereignty: build it yourself, on your own terms, at your own pace. A member of the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe and once the youngest person elected to its tribal council, Cody founded the organization in 2017 after the NoDAPL protests and went on to develop North Dakota’s largest solar farm. It recently became an independent 501(c)(3) this spring and now runs energy makeover projects in four tribal communities and food sovereignty work in two more. A household that signs on returns a share of what it saves — a flat fee on one reservation, a 50-50 split on another, a sliding scale elsewhere that asks 70% from a family with a job and 10% or 20% from an elder on a fixed income. The revolving fund covers maintenance, sustains jobs, and eventually becomes collateral in places where conventional lending has never worked, because sovereign land doesn’t fit a repossession-based credit model. Cody puts it plainly: tribes are asking for a hand up, and the point of the first dollar is that nobody has to come back for a second.

The Tribal Renewable Energy Coalition that Indigenized Energy helped form won $136 million through the EPA’s $7 billion Solar for All program, and Cody’s team completed some of the earliest builds in the country before EPA terminated the program in August 2025. The reimbursements never arrived. Cody also declines to treat any of this as partisan, and he’s persuasive about why: North Dakota is a firm red state, and its farmers run the math on solar the way they’d run it on a truck loan. Ann Arbor and Portland are now standing up residential programs that resemble what tribes have operated for years, which makes Indian Country something closer to an R&D lab for community-scale energy than a charity case. The conversation gets most interesting where energy stops being about electricity. Indigenized Energy’s off-grid array at the Northern Cheyenne buffalo facility in Montana — too remote for a grid connection — runs the electric fences, water stations, and freezers, and feeds both the tribe and local food banks; Solar Builder named it 2025 Project of the Year under 100 kW. A similar system is going up at Wozu on Standing Rock to support a returning buffalo herd, doubling as shade during handling. Grass-fed buffalo raised on regenerated grassland commands a premium, so healing the land lands on the balance sheet. The energy decision, the food decision, and the land decision are one decision, because the systems were never separate to begin with.

To learn more about Indigenized Energy’s projects, programs, and workforce training, visit 4Indigenized.energy. September 16–18, 2026 brings People of the Sun to the Standing Rock Reservation, a three-day gathering of tribal leaders, energy practitioners, artists, and musicians marking ten years since NoDAPL; tickets are at PeopleoftheSun.live.

Subscribe to Sustainability In Your Ear on iTunes

Follow Sustainability In Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube

Interview Transcript

Mitch Ratcliffe 0:11

Hello. Good morning. Good afternoon. Or good evening, wherever you are on this beautiful planet of ours. Welcome to Sustainability In Your Ear. This is the podcast conversation about accelerating the transition to a sustainable, carbon-neutral society, and I’m your host, Mitch Ratcliffe. Thanks for joining the conversation today.

We’re going to be talking energy independence for the nation’s Indigenous communities, which could be a model for the distributed, community-centered electrical grid of the future. The national conversation about the clean energy transition has a geography problem. It tends to center on the places where capital flows most easily: coastal cities, utility-scale solar farms in the Sun Belt, offshore wind corridors in the Northeast. But the communities bearing the highest energy burdens in the country, many of them rural and tribal, are treated as footnotes in a story that’s mostly being written somewhere else, and that gap is getting harder to ignore.

Electricity demand is accelerating, driven by AI infrastructure buildout, data centers, and an aging grid straining to modernize, and the pressure continues to land unevenly. Native American families pay disproportionately high utility rates for disproportionately unreliable service, and they’re facing the next wave of demand pressure while the federal policy environment that was supposed to support tribal energy development has become, let’s just say, significantly less reliable. The clean energy transition, as currently structured, risks leaving behind the communities that most need it.

And my guest today has been working on the other version of this story, one in which tribes don’t wait for transitions to arrive, but build their electric infrastructure themselves, on their own terms, at their own pace. Cody Two Bears is the co-founder and CEO of Indigenized Energy, an organization that works alongside tribal nations to advance energy sovereignty. He’s a member of the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe, and what makes Indigenized Energy structurally different from most organizations in this market is the thing it refuses to be — an outside group bringing solutions to Native communities. Instead, Indigenized Energy is of these communities, staffed by Native leaders and practitioners, accountable to tribal governance, and built to stay present long after a grant cycle or a solar installation is complete.

Indigenized Energy is running energy makeover projects in four tribal communities, integrating solar, battery storage, and efficiency upgrades in a model designed to be replicable. Alongside that work, food sovereignty projects in two communities are connecting clean energy directly to land regeneration, water conservation, and community food systems, because Cody’s position is that energy is never just about electricity. And in April of this year, Indigenized Energy became an independent 501(c)(3) nonprofit, a milestone that positions the organization to steward resources directly and build the long-term partnerships its three-year strategic plan depends upon.

The strategic plan itself is worth understanding. It’s built around a vision of Native-led energy futures that support thriving communities for the next seven generations — a planning horizon that puts it in a different category than most organizations in this sector. Where others build for grant cycles, Indigenized Energy is building for continuity across leadership transitions, funding volatility, and the long arc of what it actually takes to shift who holds power in an energy system.

So we’re going to talk with Cody about what the energy burdens actually look like in the communities where he works; how AI demand growth is shaping the stakes for rural and tribal communities; why distributed solar and microgrids are tools for sovereignty and not just cost savings; how energy connects to food and land and water in ways the mainstream conversation misses; as well as what it means to build an organization from the inside out rather than delivering solutions from the outside in. The clean energy transition will either be built with Indigenous communities or it will be built around them. Let’s explore Cody Two Bears’ clear view of the difference right after this quick commercial break.

Welcome to Sustainability In Your Ear, Cody Two Bears. How you doing today?

Cody Two Bears 4:21

Good, good. Could be better, I guess. We know there’s a lot of energy crisis out there, a lot of things happening in the world. But you know, we’re doing the best we can with some of the circumstances.

Mitch Ratcliffe 4:35

What we’re going to talk about on this show — the circumstances are truly unusual. But Indigenized Energy’s vision is for a Native-led energy future that supports generations of thriving communities for the next seven generations, and that’s a very different planning horizon than, you know, say, the typical three- or five-year corporate strategy or any federal funding cycle. How does that thinking in generations change the decisions you’re making about energy today?

Cody Two Bears 4:55

I think the biggest thing — what it looks like for, you know, energy is our people. Native American people never really thought about the energy crisis like we do today. You know, 150 years ago, 200 years ago, we were always just focused on, you know, food sovereignty, our traditional medicines, our culture, our language, things that are really important to us. But now, as we fast forward and we live in this energy era, and with really climate change kind of causing catastrophic events around the world, you’re starting to see how energy can really play a role into life-and-death situations.

So because of that, Native American people now have really looked toward energy sovereignty as another pillar, just like all those other things like food sovereignty and traditional medicines. Now, as we move forward, it’s really important to really understand how energy can really heal the land, but also making sure that energy can also make us sovereign and less reliant on outside resources.

Mitch Ratcliffe 5:57

We’re going to talk a lot about the idea of sovereignty over the course of the conversation, but let me ask you about what the day-to-day experience of energy dependence feels like. What’s it actually look like in a family’s daily life?

Cody Two Bears 6:10

I think, you know, being on the reservation — I grew up on the reservation my whole life. I grew up in one of the top 10 poorest counties in the country, Sioux County, North Dakota, on the Standing Rock Reservation. Standing Rock borders two states, North Dakota and South Dakota, and is the fifth largest landmass tribe in the country. So we have a lot of land out there, rich land.

But most importantly, when I grew up, I never understood why we always had frequent power outages, why some of our utility rates were some of the highest compared to other places around us in the region especially. But I think the biggest thing I’ve learned is we always had to pay more for energy, which I would call less reliable energy. But yet we had to pay more, which was kind of ironic in itself.

But most communities, we are what I always say at the end of the line, you know, and it is something that, as infrastructure at the end of the lines are always aging, outages can be more frequent and even last longer toward the end of those utility lines. You know, that really hits our families on reservations pretty hard. The energy burden of us having to try to keep the lights on and keep our homes warm in the winter — and you can only imagine, in the northern plains where I grew up, we experience, I would say, sometimes below negative 70 wind chill factor, and we have to keep our homes warm. And could you imagine not having energy during those times, or having a power outage during those times? And now we’re even experiencing, you know, 100-plus-degree weather more frequent in the South. Could you imagine not having cool air, energy, in the South?

So, you know, those are the things that have really struck hard to a lot of people now. You know, in reservations where I come from, we’ve been experiencing those for a long time. Now we’re just seeing the rest of the world starting to experience that and start to ask questions. But I think that makes our work at Indigenized Energy even more important now, and why our energy projects have really powerful impacts, is because lowering energy costs puts real money back into our families’ pockets, especially places where I come from. But it also creates more resilient homes, which is really important. Local jobs, and of course, everlasting economic value for our community, which is really, really important. So the work that I do here at Indigenized Energy is that much more impactful because of all the reasons I just discussed, and you can walk out your front door and see the results.

Mitch Ratcliffe 8:43

And that must be a really powerful experience, not just for you, but for everybody in the community.

Cody Two Bears 8:49

Absolutely. And I think the biggest thing is taking it right off the top of the meter, having those significant savings in real time, just right there at our disposal, right off the meter. Distributed energy projects, for me, in Indian Country are really important. You know, I know just having a reliable grid is really important as well, of creating solar farms and different things like that. But you can’t create a higher, more impact than actual distributed energy in Native communities.

Mitch Ratcliffe 9:18

Let’s talk a little about sovereignty, and this is a concept that’s breaking out all over the place. On the net, for instance, it means raising walls around data and infrastructure that were previously open. It sounds like some previous historical eras — the enclosure movement in the U.K., for instance, and of course the seizing of lands from Native tribes in the United States. Can you unpack how tribal sovereignty supports your community’s relationship with place and resources? What does that mean to your community?

Cody Two Bears 9:51

I think, you know, just look at it in this aspect of it. For many generations, Native tribes have contributed a lot to the greater good of the United States, this country that we live in today. You know, we were the ones that had to give up a lot of our land, a lot of our resources, to be able to allow this country to flourish. Well, now a lot of Native lands have shrunken now because of the taking of our lands and what has happened historically in the last couple hundred years.

But I think the biggest thing that we’re learning from that is whatever little lands we have left now — because before then they threw us on lands that were some of the most ugliest, desolate lands in the country. But now they’re some of the most beautiful lands in the world, you know, because we’re able to preserve those lands that we’re on. But because of that, there’s not a lot of development happening on those lands.

So what we look at that is, you know, number one, our energy infrastructure is very outdated. Number two, we’re using more energy more and more every day. The Western world, where we live in the United States, we use energy probably 10 times more than the other side of the world, and it’s just continuously, rapidly growing. You know, our energy needs are always growing. So we as Native tribes, because we have a lot of land, we have a lot of resources, we want to be able to contribute to the energy grid in a way that we see fit, in a way of what energy sovereignty means to us. So not only do we want to take care of our own people and our own needs, so we don’t have to always outsource our needs anymore — we want to always insource our needs.

I think the creative thing that we’re doing here can teach a lot of municipalities, can teach a lot of communities out there, states, on actually how to be sovereign themselves and not have to rely on the greater good. Because one example: when we all know the catastrophic events that happened in Texas a few years ago, where they got cold down there and their grid shut down, and they had no power for like a week or a week and a half or something — and then we had to shut down up in the north for like four hours out of the day to send power down their way, you know. So those are just some of those examples why I say energy resiliency is really important, because we have to start to protect our communities and where we live, and our states and where we come from, even more so.

And that’s what energy sovereignty and what Indigenized Energy is doing for tribes. We’re starting to work with tribes to help them become their own tribal utility, help them generate their own energy in their own communities, and help dictate those rates, because we know that with all the things that are going on in the world right now — and we’ll get into a lot of the AI data center stuff here in a minute — but because of all the energy needs and what’s happening in the world right now, those energy costs are continuously rising. So it’s really important to be able to generate our own energy moving forward, and that’s what energy sovereignty means to Native Americans.

But what I will say is, energy is different for every tribe. There’s 575 federally recognized tribes, and they might do it a little bit different compared to the next tribe, and that’s okay. But the most important part is looking out for their future generations to come and making sure that those energy costs stay within those communities, but most importantly, they’re more resilient than ever. As you know, solar is becoming cheaper every single year. It’s becoming cheaper, it’s more efficient. So tribes are really looking at a way in to figure out how can we become more energy sovereign as we move forward.

Mitch Ratcliffe 13:20

As you say, distributed energy and microgrids can offer real energy independence, but there’s a lot of upfront costs and technical complexity. What’s the model for making these projects work financially for communities that have been systematically under-resourced?

Cody Two Bears 13:36

And that’s a great question there, Mitch, because, you know, Indigenized Energy — I really thought hard about this. We all know that in the world today we can put up an energy project in a community like mine and turn around and leave. But if you don’t build the skill sets or capacity internally to make them understand what you’re putting there, it’s probably going to fail. It’s probably going to crumble in the next two or three years, right?

So what I do is, we create projects at Indigenized Energy, but we also are really focused on creating programs. And within those programs — what I’ve learned, and Indigenized Energy has been doing this for the last seven years now, Mitch, which is pretty exciting — so what we do is, when we create a program, a distributed residential solar program for a tribe, usually we need some upfront capital or philanthropic dollars to help kickstart a program, but knowing that these programs are going to be self-sufficient on their own.

So what I mean by that, Mitch: if you had a home on the reservation and you wanted solar, and you wanted to sign up for a program, you could sign up for that program. Every program that we create is different from each reservation that we work with. One reservation would have a flat rate fee that you would pay to be a part of the program. Another one saves 50% across the board on what you generate in your home and what you save in your home in that month — 50% of that goes back into the program, the other 50 you get to save within your own pocket. Or another one is like a sliding scale, right? Where if you work, which is, you know, very rare on reservations, not a whole lot of jobs, but if you do work, you might be able to pay up to 70% back into the program. You keep 30% because you have a job, while there’s other elders that are on fixed incomes that may only pay 10 or 20% back. So it’s like a sliding scale program.

And what we’re seeing with these programs — and that’s what we got awarded Solar for All, the $7 billion through the EPA, Mitch. Everybody comes from somewhere, and everybody once had a way of living that worked where they lived. We’ve been moving an awful lot compared to previous generations the last few centuries. Every region has its own climate, its own growing season, its own medicines, its own history of how the land came to be what it is, and every place contains a community: human, animal, insect, fungal, and many more. Nature is a set of systems, and we are inside them. We’re not standing over them, as Western thinking has insisted for centuries, even as the systems that we depend upon collapse.

Native and Indigenous peoples have carried and transmitted the practical knowledge that we need about how to live as part of those systems rather than at their expense, and that is the inheritance available to all of us. But we have to learn to be of and for our place, not just prepared to take from it, whether we’re talking about mining the land, cultures, or personal data for profit. And there’s no cookie-cutter approach to designing a sustainable future that can succeed, which means the transferable asset in this case isn’t a solar project design, but the practice of knowing where you are well enough to know what to build.

Cody’s grandmother’s line lands harder than any policy paper that you’re ever going to read: you don’t know where you’re going unless you know where you’ve been. And you can see that principle at work in the Northern Cheyenne buffalo facility, an off-grid array that drew national attention to Indigenized Energy’s work in the tribal energy sector. The installation runs the electric fences, the water stations, the freezers, and it helps feed the community and local food banks. Energy sovereignty and food sovereignty aren’t parallel programs; they’re the same program, and a larger system than either one alone. Grass-fed buffalo raised on regenerated grassland commands a premium price, which adds the benefit of healing the land to the buffalo meat operation’s bottom line.

And that’s what balance looks like when it’s working. The energy decision, the food decision, the land decision, along with the economic decisions, are one decision, because the systems were never separate to begin with. But we’ve consistently oversimplified the economic thinking that we do. However, systems thinking is starting to unpack that misunderstanding.

Now compare all of that to the other thread in this conversation — the one I can’t shake. Cody did not romanticize the AI data center buildout. He said it’s coming, and there’s no avoiding it. He explained the mechanism driving higher energy prices plainly: rising regional demand, rising rates, and when load has to be curtailed so that you can feed those data centers, the end of the line is where the curtailment lands. Standing Rock is the fifth largest tribal landmass in the country, and also one of the poorest counties in America, and residents already pay premium prices for less reliable service. A 2023 Department of Energy report documented this pattern. Where the wire ends tells you who mattered when it was built. And the thing we have to recognize about building an infrastructure is it hardwires these power relationships over the long term — not just energy power, but political and social power.

But consider the sense of resignation in that statement, that there’s no avoiding it. A new set of systems arrives, sited by people far away, and the community absorbs the consequences without a seat at the table. And look, Cody’s people have lived that story before at a scale that most of us have never had to imagine, and here we are laying another vast layer of infrastructure on top of an industrial system that is already tearing nature apart, and we’re calling its arrival inevitable. It’s not just Native communities on the receiving end of these dictates that will shape their future — it’s all of us, and that’s the part we need to refuse. We need a voice at the table. A sense of inevitability, in the way that Cody expressed it, is the experience of a decision someone made and declined to explain to you. We can have a real conversation about what to adopt, what to discard, and what our choices mean, but only if we understand the whole system in which we are living, considering everyone and everything in the system — which is exactly the understanding indigenizing our thinking would provide.

“End of the line” speaks volumes. It suggests we’re on our own, although when you get there, you actually find that there are communities everywhere in need of new infrastructure. We’re all at the end of the line in the take, make, and waste centralized economy. We have to recognize that, which brings me to the practical answer that Cody would hand to any rural county commissioner in America.

Indigenized Energy doesn’t just install systems; it builds programs with communities that fund themselves. When a household signs on to get solar installation, it returns a share of what it saves — perhaps a flat fee here, a 50-50 split in the cost there, a sliding scale that asks for 70% from a working family and only 10% or 20% from an elder who’s living on a fixed income. The community is in it together, and that revolving fund pays for maintenance, sustains jobs, and eventually becomes collateral where conventional lending has never worked, because sovereign land doesn’t fit a repo-based credit model. It’s an acknowledgement that everyone in the community is in it together.

The Tribal Renewable Energy Coalition that Indigenized Energy helped form won 136 million dollars through the EPA’s Solar for All program on the strength of its community-centric design. Then the federal money was pulled mid-planning, and Cody’s organization ate unreimbursed costs on the way out. But the model didn’t fail, because the communities were bought into its benefits. It was the funding cycle that failed. It was the political system that failed.

Meanwhile, cities like Ann Arbor and Portland are building residential distributed programs that look a lot like what tribes have been running for years, and that’s your leading indicator. Indian Country is the R&D lab for community-scale energy, and it still needs philanthropic or public capital to kickstart — capital that’s scarcer than it was 18 months ago.

We can learn a lot from Indigenized Energy. The communities that were never at the center of the grid may be the ones who figure out how to live well without depending on it, because they never lost the habit of asking what a place can actually support. Think local, act local, and understand our roles — and there are many roles — in the larger world that we owe our children and grandchildren, down to the seventh generation.

September at Standing Rock marks 10 years since the NoDAPL protests. A decade has turned that protest into a renewable energy infrastructure. Indigenize what you build where you are. That’s the plan. We’ll be tracking what gets built next, and the growing awareness that becoming small-i indigenous is the challenge of our times. So get to know nature and the human systems that shape your reality.

Hey, look, if this conversation gave you something worth passing along, please send it to one person who needs to hear it, or leave a review where you listen. Folks, you’re the amplifiers that can spread more ideas to create less waste, and our archive has more than 560 episodes, and it’s waiting for you on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, iHeartRadio, Audible, or whatever purveyor of podcast goodness you prefer. Thanks for your support.

I’m Mitch Ratcliffe. This is Sustainability In Your Ear, and we will be back with another Innovator interview soon. In the meantime, folks, take care of yourself, take care of one another, and let’s all take care of this beautiful planet of ours. Have a green day.

articles.personality_title: Indigenized Energy empowers Native communities with solar sovereignty and resilience
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many Native American households in the U.S. face higher energy costs and more frequent power outages than the national average. About 17,000 tribal homes still lack electricity. This problem worsens as new demands from technology like AI data centers increase pressure on the power grid. Traditional energy systems often leave tribal communities at the “end of the line” with unreliable service and high bills.

**What happened** – Cody Two Bears, CEO of Indigenized Energy and a member of the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe, founded a Native-led nonprofit focused on tribal energy sovereignty. Since 2017, Indigenized Energy has developed solar, battery storage, and efficiency projects in multiple tribal communities. They design programs where households share savings in ways that fit each community’s needs, creating funds to maintain systems and sustain jobs. Their projects also connect energy to food sovereignty and land regeneration, like powering buffalo facilities off-grid.

**Impact** – Indigenized Energy’s approach centers tribal leadership and adapts to local cultures and climates. This has made energy more affordable, reliable, and sustainable for Native families, often in some of the poorest and most remote areas. Their model creates community-owned programs that can fund themselves, overcoming barriers like lack of conventional loans on sovereign land. The organization’s work has been nationally recognized and serves as a model for other rural and marginalized communities.

**What's next step** – Indigenized Energy recently became an independent nonprofit, allowing it to grow and deepen partnerships. Though federal funding was cut unexpectedly, the group seeks new resources to expand their programs. They will host the People of the Sun gathering in September 2026 at Standing Rock, marking ten years since the NoDAPL protests and celebrating Indigenous leadership in energy, food, and culture.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By building community-driven solar and energy programs rooted in tribal sovereignty, Indigenized Energy is creating resilient, affordable power that supports Native communities’ culture, economy, and environment.

articles.title: Sustainable Pavers for Cold Climates
articles.summary:
Every construction or home improvement project is a little more complicated for people who live...
The post Sustainable Pavers for Cold Climates appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Every construction or home improvement project is a little more complicated for people who live in climates with severe winters. You just have to plan a little harder — and sometimes pay a little more — to come up with solutions to potential problems from ice dams to frost heave.

Water finds every flaw. It seeps into a hairline pore in a concrete paver, freezes overnight, expands roughly 9 percent, and levers the material apart from the inside. Repeat that cycle dozens of times over a single Northern or Midwestern winter, and a driveway that looked solid in October is flaking and heaving by April.

For homeowners in cold regions, the durability problem and the environmental problem share a single answer: pavers that let meltwater drain through the surface instead of pooling on top and refreezing. Since we first published this guide, the category has grown up. Several manufacturers now sell cold-climate-rated systems, some ship straight to your door, and researchers have built the first permeable concrete engineered specifically to survive frost. Here is what holds up when the temperature drops, and where to buy it.

Two Ways Winter Breaks Pavement

Spall is the cracking and flaking of the paver. It is mostly a problem in porous materials like concrete and brick, though it can happen in natural stone. The industry measures this resistance, publishing the ASTM C1645 specification describing the freeze-thaw and de-icing-salt durabilityfor concrete interlocking pavers, and ASTM C67, which covers clay pavers. If a product is rated to those standards, the manufacturer has tested it against exactly the conditions a cold-climate winter delivers.

Frost heave is the second threat, and it comes from below. When soil under a paver freezes, the water in it expands and pushes the paver up out of place. The traditional fix was to seal the surface completely so no water could get underneath. We now know that shedding all that runoff carries its own costs to streams and groundwater, so the better goal is to keep rainwater and snowmelt on site while still managing the freeze. A properly designed permeable system can do both.

Eco-conscious homeowners often want to skip sealants, since petroleum-based coatings defeat their sustainability goals. The good news is that even nonporous paving materials can be installed to drain: fill the joints between units with open-graded aggregate rather than mortar, and water passes around each paver into the ground below.

Why Permeable Pavers Handle Cold Better

Permeable pavement can be safer in freezing weather than a solid surface. Research on cold-climate installations shows permeable pavements freeze more slowly and thaw more quickly than conventional pavement, which reduces the need for de-icer. Meltwater drains into the base instead of sitting on top, so it cannot refreeze into the slick sheet of ice that forms on a sealed driveway. The trade-off you have to plan for is frost heave, and that is where the material choice matters most.

Flexible plastic grid systems have a structural advantage here. Their ring-and-grid design flexes with seasonal soil movement instead of cracking, and the large void space lets snowmelt flow straight through. Rigid units, by contrast, can work loose at their corners with each freeze-thaw cycle. Interlocking concrete pavers land in between: they are rigid, but their segmented joints tolerate some heave that would crack a poured slab, and pervious concrete can be mixed to resist freeze-thaw as long as the water table sits low enough.

Editor’s note: Earth911 teams up with affiliate marketing partners to help fund our Recycling Directory. If you purchase an item through one of the affiliate links in this post, we will receive a small commission.

Plastic Grid Pavers: The Cold-Climate Workhorses

Open-cell plastic grids are the most freeze-proof option on this list. They are not pavers in the traditional sense but a cellular structure, usually made from recycled plastic, that you fill with gravel or grass. The flexibility that lets them shrug off frost heave is built into the material.

TRUEGRID

TRUEGRID makes its grids from 100 percent post-consumer recycled HDPE, with domestic supply and manufacturing. The company rates the pavers for a temperature range of -58°F to 194°F, and the company’s patented flex joints are designed to move with freeze-thaw cycles and expansive soils rather than crack.

Because water drains straight through, ice cannot build on the surface, and the gravel-filled cells add winter traction. TRUEGRID sells several residential lines directly through Amazon, including the lighter-duty PRO LITE driveway paver and the walkway-and-patio DECO paver. Both are can withstand snowplow damage and, the company says, carry a service life measured in decades.

Vodaland EasyPave and HexPave

A newer entry to the DIY market, Vodaland’s EasyPave grid, is molded from 100 percent recycled PPE plastic and uses a snap-and-lock connection that installs without tools. It is rated for heavy loads when filled and set on a proper base, and its lighter cousin, the HexPave system, are suitable for patios and walking paths. As with any grid, cold-climate performance depends on the base: a well-compacted, open-graded stone base with geotextile fabric is what carries the load and moves water away before it can freeze underfoot.

Invisible Structures

Invisible Structures makes a comparable product that ships in flexible rolls of recycled HDPE rather than rigid blocks. Grasspave2 can be filled with soil and turf; Gravelpave2 holds decorative aggregate. The company reports the systems have roughly 92 percent void space, so meltwater drains almost immediately, and their flexible design withstands freeze-thaw cycles without cracking or heaving. They are also plowable, provided you keep a metal blade about three-quarters of an inch above the surface or use a rubber-edged blade. These grids are the ones you see supporting fire lanes and even parking under snow load, so a residential driveway is well within their range.

Permeable Concrete Pavers: Techo-Bloc Aquastorm

If you want the look of a finished concrete driveway rather than a gravel-filled grid, Techo-Bloc’s permeable line is built for the North American freeze-thaw belt. Their flagship Aquastorm paver is dry-cast concrete with integrated spacers that hold a consistent joint for drainage. Techo-Bloc rates it as both freeze-thaw resistant and de-icing-salt resistant, and its published infiltration rate clears water fast enough to eliminate surface pooling. The paver meets ASTM C1319 for concrete grid paving units and carries a limited lifetime warranty. Color runs all the way through each unit, so winter wear and de-icer exposure will not strip the surface tone.

GraniteCrete, the concrete alternative

GraniteCrete, which we featured in the original article, is made using a decomposed-granite admixture. It remains a strong permeable choice, but the maker still advises against it for cold climates: prolonged freezing can damage the surface, so it is best kept to moderate regions. It is now sold through building-materials distributors well beyond its original California and Texas footprint.

Recycled Rubber Tiles

Rubber pavers made from used tires solve a genuine waste problem and stay comfortable underfoot in the cold. Rubberific’s dual-sided recycled rubber pavers install in minutes over a hard surface and are widely available on Amazon. In Canada, the Envirotile line from Multy Home sells through Home Depot and is promoted as weather- and frost-resistant, with no need to lift the tiles for winter.

Keep two cautions in mind when shopping for rubber tiles. Most are designed to lay over an existing hard surface, and in that configuration they are not permeable, so they do not deliver the drainage benefit that helps grids and permeable concrete in winter. And because they are made from used tires, the material can carry residual materials that can be harmful to the environment. Keep rubber pavers away from vegetable gardens and other food crops, where tire-derived compounds could leach into soil you eat from.

On the Horizon: Frost-Proof Permeable Concrete

Conventional pervious concrete has always struggled in the cold, deteriorating under frost and clogging with sediment over time. That may be changing. Materials researchers have developed a clogging-resistant permeable concrete that the study describes as the first high-strength permeable pavement durable under frost action without the air-entrainment additives normally required. The work is still moving from the lab toward commercial use, so it is not something you can order yet. But it points at a future where a poured permeable driveway performs in Minnesota as reliably as a plastic grid does today. We will update this guide as products reach the market.

What You Can Do

Choosing a cold-climate paver comes down to matching the material to your site and your winter:

For a driveway that faces heavy snow and hard freezes, start with a recycled-plastic grid, such as TRUEGRID, Vodaland, or Invisible Structures filled with open-graded gravel. Flexibility beats rigidity when the ground moves.

If you want a finished concrete look, specify a permeable paver rated to ASTM C1645 for freeze-thaw and de-icing-salt durability, such as Techo-Bloc Aquastorm. The rating is your proof the product was tested for your climate.

Invest in the base, not just the surface. Most cold-climate paver failures trace to the sub-base. An open-graded stone base over geotextile fabric moves water away before it freezes and is what carries the load.

Skip petroleum sealants on porous pavers; they defeat the drainage that keeps ice from forming. Choose a system that drains by design instead.

Keep recycled-rubber and tire-based products away from vegetable beds and edible landscaping.

Before you disturb an old surface, check whether the material can be reused. Repurposed wood and salvaged brick make handsome patios, and the greenest paver is often the one you do not have to buy. For disposal of what you remove, use the Earth911 Recycling Search to find local options.

Related Reading

Editor’s Note: Originally published by Gemma Alexander on July 8, 2019, this article was substantially updated in July 2026.

The detailed coverage of materials, standards, and future research provides substantial context and significance.
articles.personality_title: New sustainable pavers help cold-climate homes manage winter weather
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Building driveways and patios in places with cold winters is tricky. Water can get into cracks in paving stones, freeze, and cause damage. This happens again and again during winter, making surfaces crack and lift.

**What happened** – Companies and researchers have developed new types of pavers that let water drain through instead of pooling on top. These include plastic grid systems made from recycled materials, permeable concrete pavers made for cold weather, and recycled rubber tiles. These products are designed to handle freeze-thaw cycles better, avoid ice buildup, and last for many years in cold climates.

**Impact** – These new pavers reduce damage caused by winter weather and help manage water runoff in an eco-friendly way. For example, plastic grids flex with soil movement instead of cracking, and permeable concrete lets water pass through to avoid pooling ice. This means safer, longer-lasting surfaces for homeowners without harming the environment.

**What's next step** – Researchers are working on frost-proof permeable concrete that could offer even stronger, durable options in the future. Homeowners can already choose from tested products like TRUEGRID, Techo-Bloc Aquastorm, and others that meet cold-climate standards. Proper installation, especially the base under the pavers, is important to ensure success.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New permeable and flexible pavers made from recycled materials provide durable, eco-friendly solutions for managing winter weather in cold regions.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouveaux pavés durables aident les maisons des climats froids à affronter l’hiver
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Construire une allée ou une terrasse dans les régions froides est compliqué. L’eau s’infiltre dans les fissures des pavés, gèle, puis cause des dégâts en se dilatant. Ce processus répété abîme les surfaces pendant l’hiver.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des entreprises et chercheurs ont créé des pavés qui laissent l’eau s’écouler à travers eux au lieu de stagner à la surface. Il s’agit de systèmes en grille plastique recyclée, de pavés en béton perméable adaptés au froid, et de dalles en caoutchouc recyclé. Ces produits résistent mieux au gel et au dégel, évitent la formation de glace et durent longtemps.

**Impact** – Ces pavés innovants réduisent les dommages liés à l’hiver et améliorent la gestion écologique des eaux de pluie. Par exemple, les grilles plastiques fléchissent avec le sol sans se fissurer, et le béton perméable laisse passer l’eau pour éviter la glace. Cela offre aux propriétaires des surfaces plus sûres et durables, sans nuire à l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Des chercheurs développent un béton perméable résistant au gel pour des options encore plus solides. Les propriétaires peuvent déjà choisir des produits testés comme TRUEGRID ou Techo-Bloc Aquastorm, en veillant à bien préparer la base sous les pavés.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouveaux pavés perméables et flexibles en matériaux recyclés offrent des solutions durables et écologiques pour affronter l’hiver dans les régions froides.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos pavimentos sostenibles ayudan a hogares en climas fríos a enfrentar el invierno
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Construir entradas o patios en lugares con inviernos fríos es difícil. El agua se filtra en las grietas de los pavimentos, se congela y causa daños al expandirse. Esto ocurre repetidamente durante el invierno, dañando las superficies.

**Qué pasó** – Empresas e investigadores han desarrollado nuevos pavimentos que permiten que el agua se drene a través de ellos en lugar de acumularse en la superficie. Incluyen sistemas de rejillas plásticas hechas de materiales reciclados, pavimentos de concreto permeable diseñados para el frío y losetas de caucho reciclado. Estos productos resisten mejor los ciclos de congelación y descongelación, evitan la formación de hielo y duran muchos años.

**Impacto** – Estos nuevos pavimentos reducen los daños causados por el invierno y ayudan a manejar el agua de lluvia de forma ecológica. Por ejemplo, las rejillas plásticas se flexionan con el movimiento del suelo sin quebrarse, y el concreto permeable permite que el agua pase para evitar charcos de hielo. Esto brinda superficies más seguras y duraderas para los hogares sin dañar el medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores trabajan en concreto permeable a prueba de heladas para opciones aún más resistentes en el futuro. Los propietarios ya pueden elegir productos probados como TRUEGRID o Techo-Bloc Aquastorm, asegurándose de preparar bien la base debajo del pavimento.

**Resumen en una frase** – Nuevos pavimentos permeables y flexibles hechos con materiales reciclados ofrecen soluciones duraderas y ecológicas para manejar el invierno en regiones frías.

articles.reasoning: The article reports on the creation of high-resolution maps of European wetlands using satellite images and machine learning, which significantly aids conservation efforts by revealing previously overlooked wetland patches. This represents a meaningful advancement with broad environmental impact and sufficient substance to be considered inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: New detailed maps reveal hidden wetlands across Europe
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Wetlands are areas where water covers the land, like marshes and swamps. These places are important because they provide homes for many plants and animals and help clean water. But some wetlands are small or hard to spot, so they can be missed on maps.

**What happened** – Scientists used satellite pictures of Europe and a type of computer program called machine learning to study the images. This helped them create very detailed maps of wetlands, showing even small or hidden areas that were not known before. The research was published in July 2026.

**Impact** – These new maps help people who protect nature by showing exactly where wetlands are. Finding previously unknown wetlands means they can be cared for and kept safe. This is important because wetlands help stop floods, store carbon, and support wildlife.

**What's next step** – Conservation groups and governments can now use these maps to plan better ways to protect wetlands. The technology might also be used in other parts of the world to find hidden wetlands and improve care for these special places.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Advanced satellite mapping has uncovered hidden European wetlands, giving new tools to protect these valuable ecosystems.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles cartes détaillées révèlent des zones humides cachées en Europe
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les zones humides sont des endroits où l'eau couvre la terre, comme les marais. Elles sont importantes car elles abritent de nombreuses plantes et animaux et aident à purifier l'eau. Mais certaines zones humides sont petites ou difficiles à repérer, donc elles peuvent être oubliées sur les cartes.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé des images satellite de l'Europe et un programme informatique appelé apprentissage automatique pour étudier ces images. Cela leur a permis de créer des cartes très précises des zones humides, montrant même des zones petites ou cachées qui n'étaient pas connues auparavant. Cette recherche a été publiée en juillet 2026.

**Impact** – Ces nouvelles cartes aident les personnes qui protègent la nature en montrant exactement où se trouvent les zones humides. Trouver des zones humides inconnues signifie qu'elles peuvent être protégées. C’est important car les zones humides aident à prévenir les inondations, stocker le carbone et soutenir la faune.

**Prochaine étape** – Les groupes de conservation et les gouvernements peuvent maintenant utiliser ces cartes pour mieux protéger les zones humides. Cette technologie pourrait aussi être utilisée dans d'autres régions du monde pour découvrir des zones humides cachées et mieux les préserver.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La cartographie satellite avancée a révélé des zones humides cachées en Europe, offrant de nouveaux outils pour protéger ces écosystèmes précieux.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevos mapas detallados revelan humedales ocultos en Europa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los humedales son áreas donde el agua cubre la tierra, como pantanos y ciénagas. Son importantes porque brindan hogar a muchas plantas y animales y ayudan a limpiar el agua. Pero algunos humedales son pequeños o difíciles de ver, por eso pueden no aparecer en los mapas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos usaron imágenes satelitales de Europa y un programa de computadora llamado aprendizaje automático para estudiar las imágenes. Esto les permitió crear mapas muy detallados de humedales, mostrando áreas pequeñas o escondidas que antes no se conocían. La investigación se publicó en julio de 2026.

**Impacto** – Estos nuevos mapas ayudan a quienes protegen la naturaleza al mostrar dónde están los humedales con precisión. Encontrar humedales desconocidos significa que pueden ser cuidados y protegidos. Esto es importante porque los humedales ayudan a evitar inundaciones, almacenan carbono y apoyan la vida silvestre.

**Próximo paso** – Los grupos de conservación y gobiernos pueden usar estos mapas para planear mejores formas de proteger los humedales. La tecnología también podría usarse en otras partes del mundo para encontrar humedales ocultos y mejorar su cuidado.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La avanzada cartografía satelital ha descubierto humedales ocultos en Europa, proporcionando nuevas herramientas para proteger estos valiosos ecosistemas.
